Ch1_HGLMM_Late season influence of beaver dams on surface water area

The purpose of this script is to explore the effect of beaver dams on surface water area, relative to local geomorphology, and contributing drainage area. This modeling focuses solely on late summer months; August : September.

Two models are presented herein. Both models have the same random effects structure for stream, year, and sample event. Model 1 examines the effect of beaver dam presence on surface water area, with no consideration for local context. Model 2 includes additional fixed effects of channel incision, riparian area, and drainage area.

Data from USGS LBV Guston Coulee gage, near Saco, MT:

Late Summer Flow Conditions

2019:

Moderate August with peak in September

2021:

Low flow conditions throughout the summer season. Part of 3 year sustained drought conditions (2020, 2021, 2022).

Gage recorded no flow during August & September

2023:

Moderate to low August & September flows; after early season extreme flooding.

GEE-derived data for each stream:

Currently each incoming data frame is of the following structure:

Where:

- clusterID_seq = dammed/undammed sample pair ID
- window_type = whether a sample window is 'dammed' or 'undammed'
- watershed = watershed identifier (e.g., "BRZ" = Brazil Ck)
- year = year
- window_length = length (m) of stream flow line within sample window
- catchment_km2 = contributing catchment area/upstream drainage area (km^2)
- riparian_area_m2 = riparian area (m^2) of sample window; 2-m vertical
  buffer from DEM low point (stream flow line)
- TO = mean topographic openness across window
- p_window: avg dam detection prob for target window; see CP Ch1
- f_window: avg dam false positive prob for target window; see CP Ch1
- beav_count_all: all unique detected dams
- beav_count_conf: unique confidently detected dams
- anthro_count: all unique detected anthropogenic dams
- isAnthro: National Wetland Inventory (NWI) "dyked/impounded"
- Nhat = estimated dam count within sample window
  - ...= beav_count_all/(p_window(1-f_window)  [McCallum 2005]
- TO_mean = mean topographic openness across window
- time_period = 2wk interval 06/01 - 09/30
- SWA = surface water area (m^2) for window during time (t)
- t = time (t) {same as time_period}

—-

—-

Relative flow data.

NOTE: currently this represents data from the USGS Guston Coulee gage that has been z-standardized over the current data acquisition range (June-Sept; 2019, 2021, 2023).

Then, for each 2-week survey time period (1:8), the average relative flow \([mean(zFlow)]\) was computed, and added as a data column. IT IS ASSUMED that this flow data is representative of the RELATIVE flow pattern experienced throughout the study area during this time.

Add relFlow data:

Hierarchical Generalized Linear Mixed Effects Models

Proposed Models:

Both models:

\[ SWA \sim N(\mu, \sigma);\]

M1:

\[\mu = \alpha + \eta_{stream} + \eta_{event} + \eta_{cluster} + (\beta1+\beta_{streamEvent})*damPresence \]

—-

M2:

\[\mu = \alpha + \eta_{stream} + \eta_{event} + \eta_{cluster} + (\beta_1+\beta_{streamEvent})*damPresence + \beta_2 * Incision + \beta_3 * riparianArea + \beta_4 * drainageArea\]

—-

This section explores late season SWA in relation to beaver dam presence.

Uses nested random slopes for beaver dam effect to allow for stream, year, and sampleEvent to cause deviation from the global mean beaver effect.

Implement HGLMMs:

M3: Dam Presence Only; seasonal random slope (sampleEvent)

Sys.time()

sink("HGLMM_damPres.jags")

cat("
model {

  for (i in 1:N) {
    SWA[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)

  mu[i] <- alpha
             + (beta1
                + b_stream_event[stream[i],sampleEvent[i]]) * damPresence[i]
             + eta_stream[stream[i]]
             + eta_event[sampleEvent[i]]
             + eta_cluster[clusterID[i]]
  }

  # Fixed effects
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)
  beta1 ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)

  # Random Effects
  # Stream/Watershed
  for (j in 1:n_stream) {
    eta_stream[j] ~ dnorm(0, tau_stream)     # random intercept for stream
    b_stream[j]   ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_stream)   # random slope for stream
  }
  
  # Year-level dam effect
  for(yr in 1:n_year) {
    b_year[yr] ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_year)        # random slope for year
  }
  
  # Event-level dam effect nested within year
  for (k in 1:n_event) {
    b_event[k] ~ dnorm(b_year[event_year[k]],tau_b_event)       # random slope for year/survey 'sampling event'
    eta_event[k] ~ dnorm(0, tau_event)       # random intercept for event
  }
  
  # Stream/Event Interaction
  for(j in 1:n_stream){
    for(k in 1:n_event){
      b_stream_event[j,k] ~ dnorm(b_event[k] + b_stream[j], tau_b_stream_event)
    }
  }
  
  # Cluster ID
  for (c in 1:n_cluster) {
    eta_cluster[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau_cluster)    # random intercept for sampleCluster
  }

  # Hyperpriors
  sigma ~ dnorm(0, 1 / pow(sd_SWA, 2)) T(0,)   # half-normal with sd ≈ sd_SWA
  tau <- pow(sigma, -2)

  sigma_stream ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_stream <- pow(sigma_stream, -2)

  sigma_b_stream ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_b_stream <- pow(sigma_b_stream, -2)
  
  sigma_b_year ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_b_year <- pow(sigma_b_year, -2)

  sigma_b_event ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_b_event <- pow(sigma_b_event, -2)
  
  sigma_event ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_event <- pow(sigma_event, -2)
  
  sigma_b_stream_event ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_b_stream_event <- pow(sigma_b_stream_event, -2)
  
  sigma_cluster ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_cluster <- pow(sigma_cluster, -2)
}
", fill = TRUE)

sink()
## 
## Processing function input....... 
## 
## Done. 
##  
## Beginning parallel processing using 3 cores. Console output will be suppressed.
## 
## Parallel processing completed.
## 
## Calculating statistics....... 
## 
## Done.
## [1] "2026-06-19 17:15:49 MDT"

difference in sys.time = elapsed time for model to run

## [1] 180.9665

^ Average dam effect across the study area is 180.5 This translates to:

“On average, dammed reaches pond about 180 m^2 more surface water than undammed reaches.”

Compare b1 trajectory across event, by stream:

M4: Dam Presence + Context with updated random effects structure

library(jagsUI)

Sys.time()

sink("HGLMM_allCovar.jags")

cat("
    model {

  for (i in 1:N) {
    SWA[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], tau)

  mu[i] <- alpha
             + (beta1
                + b_stream_event[stream[i],sampleEvent[i]]) * damPresence[i]
             + beta2 * TO[i] 
             + beta3 * RIPA[i]
             + beta4 * UDA[i]
             + eta_stream[stream[i]]
             + eta_event[sampleEvent[i]]
             + eta_cluster[clusterID[i]]
  }

  # Fixed effects
  alpha ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)
  beta1 ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)
  beta2 ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)
  beta3 ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)
  beta4 ~ dnorm(0, 0.001)

  # Random Effects
  # Stream/Watershed
  for (j in 1:n_stream) {
    eta_stream[j] ~ dnorm(0, tau_stream)     # random intercept for stream
    b_stream[j]   ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_stream)   # random slope for stream
  }
  
  # Year-level dam effect
  for(yr in 1:n_year) {
    b_year[yr] ~ dnorm(0, tau_b_year)        # random slope for year
  }
  
  # Event-level dam effect nested within year
  for (k in 1:n_event) {
    b_event[k] ~ dnorm(b_year[event_year[k]],tau_b_event)       # random slope for year/survey 'sampling event'
    eta_event[k] ~ dnorm(0, tau_event)
  }
  
  # Stream/Event Interaction
  for(j in 1:n_stream){
    for(k in 1:n_event){
      b_stream_event[j,k] ~ dnorm(b_event[k] + b_stream[j], tau_b_stream_event)
    }
  }
  
  # Cluster ID
  for (c in 1:n_cluster) {
    eta_cluster[c] ~ dnorm(0, tau_cluster)
  }

  # Hyperpriors
  sigma ~ dnorm(0, 1 / pow(sd_SWA, 2)) T(0,)   # half-normal with sd ≈ sd_SWA
  tau <- pow(sigma, -2)

  sigma_stream ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_stream <- pow(sigma_stream, -2)

  sigma_b_stream ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_b_stream <- pow(sigma_b_stream, -2)
  
  sigma_b_year ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_b_year <- pow(sigma_b_year, -2)

  sigma_b_event ~ dunif(0, 10)
  tau_b_event <- pow(sigma_b_event, -2)
  
  sigma_event ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_event <- pow(sigma_event, -2)
  
  sigma_b_stream_event ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_b_stream_event <- pow(sigma_b_stream_event, -2)
  
  sigma_cluster ~ dunif(0,10)
  tau_cluster <- pow(sigma_cluster, -2)
}
", fill = TRUE)

sink()

difference in sys.time = elapsed time for model to run

—-

NOTE:

TO was transformed to “channel incision” by taking the inverse of proportion openness:

\[ChannelIncision = 1 - (TO/90) \]

Where \((TO/90)\) is the proportion of max openness (90 degrees = “flat”/“fully open”)

—-

m4 Mean Dam Effect:

m4 Mean TO Effect

Interpretation: SWA(m^2) change per 1 standard deviation increase in channel incision

NOTE: Blue line = mean() of posterior estimate for proportional change
      Dashed lines = 95% CI

typical dam effect = (+)~185m^2 SWA compared to undammed. Typical baseline SWA condition = ~134m^2. Hence, near doubling when we look at proportional change.
